conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let me start by asking: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'm inquiring about my daughter Alex - she's 9 years old and lives with me here in the UK. I'd like to know if I'm eligible to claim Child Benefit for her.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's helpful to know about Alex. 

Before I can assess your eligibility, I need to read the official guidance on Child Benefit rules. Let me do that now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I have the official guidance. Let me ask you a few questions to determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex, meaning you live with her full-time in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. Alex lives with me full-time here in the UK. I'm responsible for her care.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. That's good to know.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?** (For example, Alex's other parent or another family member?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. It's just me looking after her.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

**Are you or your partner (if you have one) earning over £60,000 per year individually?** (This doesn't affect whether you're eligible, but it may mean you have to pay a tax charge on the Child Benefit received.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of us earns over £60,000 a year, so that shouldn't be an issue.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One final question to ensure I have all the information I need:

**Is Alex currently in hospital, care, or any residential accommodation paid for by the local council?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in hospital or care or anything like that. She's just at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news! **You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.**

Based on the information you've provided:
- Alex is 9 years old (under 16)
- She lives with you full-time in the UK
- You are responsible for her care
- No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for her
- She is not in hospital or care

All the eligibility criteria are met. You can proceed with your Child Benefit claim for Alex.

As an additional note, since neither you nor your partner earns over £60,000 individually per year, you won't need to worry about the High Income Child Benefit tax charge.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 9 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 9 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 9 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit. According to the official guidance, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. Alex is 9 years old (under 16), lives with you full-time in the UK, and you are responsible for her care. No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, which is important as only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child. Alex is not in hospital, care, or residential accommodation. All the eligibility criteria are met for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for their 9-year-old daughter Alex. The claimant lives in the UK with Alex, is responsible for her care, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for her, and she is not in hospital or care. All eligibility requirements are satisfied. Additionally, since neither the claimant nor their partner earns over £60,000 individually, there will be no High Income Child Benefit tax charge to pay.
